source_text: sources/comments/files/20251124-5118__Indiana-Energy-Association/Indiana Energy Association Comments on FERC Docket No. RM26-4-000.txt
overall_summary: The Indiana Energy Association supports FERC's goal of enabling large load interconnections but opposes a federal rulemaking on jurisdictional grounds. Indiana asserts that states already proactively address large load issues through legislation, expedited approvals, and individualized contracts; a federal standard would be duplicative and violate the Federal Power Act's limits on FERC jurisdiction over retail service. The IEA urges FERC to preserve state authority over retail interconnections and cost recovery, and to instead focus on RTO queue reform and transmission investment.

quotes.text: The IEA supports FERC's goal of ensuring that large new loads — including industrial, manufacturing, and data-center projects — can interconnect efficiently and reliably.
quotes.concern: General support for efficient large load interconnection

quotes.text: However, as noted in NARUC's November 11, 2025 resolution on this topic, the ANOPR raises important questions about jurisdictional boundaries under the Federal Power Act ("FPA"), which sets out clear delineations of federal and state regulatory authority. Moreover, states such as Indiana are demonstrating that state procedures are proactively and effectively addressing new large load customer interconnections, rendering significant FERC intrusions into state jurisdiction unnecessary and potentially duplicative.
quotes.concern: Federal rule duplicates effective state procedures and raises jurisdictional concerns

quotes.text: Recognizes that states are proactively addressing large load customer issues. Preserves state jurisdiction over service to, and rates for, retail customers (of all magnitudes) and local distribution facilities.
quotes.concern: Explicit request to preserve state jurisdiction over retail service and rates

quotes.text: Indiana has demonstrated that it is proactively and effectively addressing large load customer issues, through legislation, unique tariffs, and expedited regulatory proceedings. For example, in 2024, the Indiana General Assembly passed legislation providing for expedited contract approvals (and expedited generation resource approvals) for large load customers, while also protecting retail customers by requiring a large load customer to provide adequate assurance of payment for eighty percent (80%) of the cost to serve such a customer.
quotes.concern: Indiana enacted expedited approval legislation and cost protections in 2024

quotes.text: In addition, for decades the IEA electric utilities have used individualized electric service contracts, approved by the IURC, to address electric interconnection and power supply to large, sophisticated customers. As noted above, the 2024 Indiana legislation also provides for expedited IURC approvals of such individualized contracts which can be customized to meet the unique needs of both the customers and the utilities. While the ANOPR asserts a desire to standardize large load customer interconnections, the IEA respectfully submits that unique contracts between a utility and its large load retail customers, scrutinized by state regulators, will better serve the large load customers, the utility, and the utility's other retail customers.
quotes.concern: Individualized state-approved contracts better than federal standardization

quotes.text: The ANOPR asserts that large load interconnections are a "critical component of open access transmission service," like electric generator interconnections standardized in Order No. 2003, requiring minimum terms and conditions to ensure non-discriminatory transmission service. There are significant differences, however, between electric generator connections and large load retail customer interconnections, particularly in traditionally-regulated, vertically-integrated states like Indiana. Not only are there longstanding federal and state jurisdictional differences between electric generator interconnections and retail customer interconnections, there are also differences in the potential for discrimination. Transmission service to independent electric generators raises legitimate questions of discrimination for competitive reasons. In contrast, in states like Indiana, utilities have both an obligation and a natural incentive to serve (and interconnect) new customers.
quotes.concern: Load interconnections fundamentally different from generator interconnections; no discrimination risk for retail customers

quotes.text: Transmission interconnections for all retail customers, regardless of size, are part and parcel of a utility's provision of retail utility service. States have adequately and efficiently regulated interconnections between their jurisdictional utilities and large load customers for decades. The only difference today is the number of large load customers — mostly data centers — seeking to interconnect and take service. A change in the number of large retail customers seeking interconnection should not drive a major change in jurisdictional boundaries between the federal government and states.
quotes.concern: Increased data-center volume should not trigger federal jurisdiction shift

quotes.text: The ANOPR also asserts that large load interconnections directly affect wholesale electricity rates, over which the FERC has exclusive jurisdiction under the FPA to ensure wholesale rates are just and reasonable. But the FERC cannot use its "affecting" jurisdiction to regulate where the Federal Power Act has expressly denied FERC jurisdiction. Notably, section 212 of the FPA provides that "[n]o order issued under this chapter shall be conditioned upon or require the transmission of electric energy: (1) directly to an ultimate customer …." 16 U.S. Code § 824k(h). Moreover, the impact of large load interconnections affects state retail electricity rates more than wholesale rates. In Indiana, the IEA electric utilities devote the vast majority of their investments and operations to serving their retail customers, not wholesale customers. States such as Indiana are experienced and well-positioned to ensure that all cost components of a utility's retail electric service, including transmission interconnections, translate into just and reasonable retail rates.
quotes.concern: FPA Section 212 prohibits conditioning transmission on retail service; retail rates more affected than wholesale

quotes.text: States — including Indiana — have long regulated the interconnection of retail customers, cost recovery for upgrades, local reliability planning, and resource adequacy. These activities fall squarely within the states' exclusive jurisdiction. Id. To maintain this balance, FERC's final rule should explicitly state that it does not apply to utility/customer interconnections involving retail service, even when those retail loads are large in magnitude.
quotes.concern: Final rule must explicitly exclude retail service from federal jurisdiction

quotes.text: Cost recovery for upgrades that serve retail load growth should remain within state regulators' ratemaking authority. Assigning such costs under federal tariffs could lead to jurisdictional conflicts and undermine established retail cost recovery mechanisms. FERC should clarify that cost-allocation rules developed in this proceeding will apply only to transmission-level network upgrades (as defined by FERC's Seven-Factor Test) associated with wholesale, not retail, transactions.
quotes.concern: Cost allocation limited to wholesale; retail cost recovery stays with states

quotes.text: The IEA supports FERC's commitment to improving the interconnection process for large loads so long as it does so in a manner that preserves the jurisdictional framework established by Congress and affirmed by the courts. The IEA submits that a cooperative federal–state approach will ensure that large-load interconnections occur efficiently, reliably, and lawfully — promoting economic growth while maintaining regulatory boundaries.
quotes.concern: Support conditional on preserving federal-state jurisdictional framework

quotes.text: Recognize that states are proactively and effectively addressing large load customer challenges, including transmission interconnections. Explicitly preserve state authority over all retail service. Ensure retail cost recovery remains under state jurisdiction. In addition, the IEA respectfully urges the FERC to continue to focus its efforts on RTO queue reform and encouraging needed transmission investment.
quotes.concern: Three-part jurisdictional recommendation; urge RTO queue and transmission investment focus

bins.name: Federal-state jurisdictional boundaries
bins.description: The IEA opposes FERC assertion of jurisdiction over retail load interconnections, arguing that the Federal Power Act limits FERC to wholesale sales and transmission in interstate commerce, explicitly excluding facilities used in local distribution. The filer contends that load interconnections for retail customers fundamentally differ from generator interconnections (no discrimination risk), that states have effectively regulated retail interconnections for decades, and that Section 212 of the FPA prohibits conditioning transmission on service to ultimate customers. The final rule must explicitly exclude retail service from federal jurisdiction to avoid conflicts with established state cost recovery mechanisms.

bins.name: Cooperative federal-state study approach
bins.description: The IEA supports FERC's goal of improving large load interconnections and favors a cooperative federal-state approach rather than federal standardization. The filer argues that Indiana's individualized contracts under state oversight better serve all parties than a one-size-fits-all federal standard. Beyond load interconnections, FERC should focus on RTO queue reform and transmission investment, areas where states lack direct regulatory authority.

bins.name: Retail cost recovery under state authority
bins.description: The IEA opposes assigning cost recovery for upgrades serving retail load growth under federal tariffs, arguing it would conflict with state ratemaking authority. Cost-allocation rules must be limited to transmission-level network upgrades associated with wholesale transactions, leaving retail cost recovery within state jurisdiction.

bins.name: Indiana state-level proactive measures
bins.description: Indiana has proactively addressed large load interconnections through 2024 legislation enabling expedited contract approvals and requiring large load customers to provide 80 percent cost-sharing assurance, protecting existing retail customers. State-approved individualized contracts have served large customers effectively for decades.
